title: Piano/Keyboard Roadmap
shortDescription: A comprehensive roadmap to learn piano from absolute beginner to advanced level.
longDescription: This roadmap guides learners from sitting at the piano for the first time through advanced repertoire. Foundational physical setup leads into reading, scales, chords, hands-together coordination, expression, sight-reading, and progressively challenging repertoire. Ear training, sight-reading, and improvisation run as parallel tracks alongside the main technical and repertoire progression. Each topic includes objectives, common mistakes, practice tips, free resources (mostly IMSLP for sheet music in the public domain), and an assessment where appropriate.

sections.title: Getting Started
sections.shortDescription: Set up your body, find your way around the keys, and produce your first sounds.
sections.longDescription: Before you read a single note, the way you sit, hold your hands, and touch the keys matters more than almost anything else. This section establishes those physical fundamentals.

topics.title: Posture, Bench Height, and Hand Position
topics.shortDescription: Set up your body and hands correctly before playing a single note.
topics.longDescription: Good posture and hand position prevent injury and let you play with control. You'll learn to sit at the correct distance and height from the keyboard, keep your shoulders relaxed, and form a natural curved hand shape with rounded knuckles.
objectives: Sit with feet flat, back relaxed but upright, and forearms parallel to the floor
Adjust the bench so your elbows are level with or slightly above the keys
Form a 'C-shape' or 'holding a ball' hand position with curved fingers
commonMistakes: Sitting too low (causes wrist tension) or too high (loses leverage)
Letting fingers collapse at the first joint
Tensing the shoulders or hunching toward the keyboard
practiceTips: Check yourself in a mirror occasionally — what feels right and what looks right aren't always the same
Take a 30-second tension scan every few minutes: shoulders, neck, wrists, jaw

questions.questionText: When seated at the piano, your forearms should be approximately:
questions.explanation: Forearms parallel to the floor (or very slightly downward toward the keys) keeps the wrists neutral and allows free finger movement.

topics.title: Finger Numbering
topics.shortDescription: The standard 1–5 numbering used in piano sheet music for both hands.
topics.longDescription: Piano music uses numbers 1 through 5 to indicate which finger plays which note. The thumb is always 1, regardless of which hand. This is universal across all piano music.
objectives: Identify each finger by its number in both hands
Recognize finger number indications in sheet music
commonMistakes: Confusing piano finger numbering with guitar (where the index finger is 1)
practiceTips: Say the finger numbers aloud as you wiggle each one — until 1=thumb is automatic

questions.questionText: On the piano, finger 1 is which finger?
questions.explanation: Finger 1 is the thumb on both hands.

topics.title: Keyboard Geography
topics.shortDescription: Locate every note on the keyboard using the black-key groupings as landmarks.
topics.longDescription: The keyboard's pattern of two- and three-black-key groups gives you visual landmarks. C is always immediately to the left of any group of two black keys. From C you can find every other note. Practice locating notes blindly to internalize the layout.
objectives: Find any natural note (C through B) anywhere on the keyboard
Locate middle C reliably
Find sharps and flats relative to natural notes
commonMistakes: Counting up from the left every time instead of using black-key landmarks
Confusing the 2-black-key group with the 3-black-key group when not looking
practiceTips: Close your eyes, name a note, then open them to check whether you put your finger on it
Practice locating notes in different octaves to build full-keyboard awareness

questions.questionText: How can you most easily locate the note C on a piano?

options.optionText: It's the white key immediately to the left of any group of two black keys

questions.explanation: Every C sits just to the left of a group of two black keys — the most reliable visual landmark on the keyboard.

topics.title: First Sounds and Touch
topics.shortDescription: Produce your first controlled sounds: even tone, weight, and release.
topics.longDescription: Pressing a piano key is more than a button-press. You'll learn to use arm weight rather than finger force, to listen for an even tone, and to release the key cleanly. This foundation underlies all future technique.
objectives: Produce a clear, even tone on a single key
Use arm weight rather than pressing with finger force alone
Release the key cleanly and intentionally
commonMistakes: Hitting the keys from too high (loses control)
'Slapping' the keys instead of pressing through them
practiceTips: Drop your hand onto a single key from a few inches up, then let the wrist relax — feel the weight transfer

questions.questionText: Good piano touch primarily uses:

options.optionText: Arm and hand weight directed through the finger

questions.explanation: Good touch uses the natural weight of the arm and hand, channeled through a supported finger — not isolated finger strength.

sections.title: Reading and First Pieces
sections.shortDescription: Read the grand staff, count rhythms, and play your first short pieces hands separately.
sections.longDescription: This section bridges from physical setup to actually playing music. You'll learn to read both clefs, count basic rhythms with a metronome, and play short five-finger position pieces hands separately.

topics.title: Reading the Grand Staff
topics.shortDescription: Piano music uses two staves — treble for the right hand, bass for the left.
topics.longDescription: Piano sheet music joins the treble and bass clef staves with a brace, forming the grand staff. Middle C sits on a ledger line between them. You'll learn to read pitches on both staves and recognize how they map to the keyboard.
objectives: Read line and space notes on the treble clef
Read line and space notes on the bass clef
Identify middle C and its position relative to both staves
commonMistakes: Reading treble clef well but neglecting bass clef daily practice
Losing track of which staff goes to which hand
practiceTips: Quiz yourself on bass clef notes as much as treble — most beginners under-practice bass
Find the same pitch on the staff and on the keyboard side by side

resources.title: The Staff, Clefs, and Ledger Lines

questions.questionText: Middle C is found:

options.optionText: On the bottom line of the treble clef
On a ledger line between the treble and bass staves
On the top line of the bass clef
On a ledger line below the bass clef

questions.explanation: Middle C sits on the first ledger line below the treble staff, which is also the first ledger line above the bass staff.

topics.title: Simple Rhythms at the Keyboard
topics.shortDescription: Count and play whole, half, quarter, and eighth notes with a steady pulse.
topics.longDescription: Reading rhythm is half of reading music. You'll practice counting common rhythmic values aloud while playing, using a metronome from day one.
objectives: Count rhythms aloud ("1-and-2-and...") while playing
Maintain a steady pulse with a metronome
Play simple rhythms in 4/4 and 3/4
commonMistakes: Rushing eighth notes (very common — the metronome catches this immediately)
Slowing down for difficult notes — keep tempo, accept wrong notes temporarily
practiceTips: Use a metronome from your very first day — it's not optional
Clap a rhythm before playing it

questions.questionText: Why is a metronome useful for beginners?

options.optionText: It plays the melody for you
It enforces a steady tempo and reveals rushing or dragging

questions.explanation: A metronome provides an objective steady pulse, exposing rushing or dragging that's invisible to the player without one.

topics.title: Five-Finger Position Pieces
topics.shortDescription: First melodies where each finger plays one note, no shifts required.
topics.longDescription: Five-finger position is the simplest hand arrangement: fingers 1–5 over five adjacent notes. Most method books start here. You'll play short pieces that build finger independence and reading skills without yet requiring hand movement.
objectives: Place your hand in C, G, and F five-finger positions
Play simple melodies hands separately in these positions
Read short pieces fluently in five-finger position
commonMistakes: Looking at the hands instead of the page
Stretching beyond the five-finger position when the music doesn't require it
practiceTips: Keep your eyes on the page — let your fingers find the keys by feel
Repeat each line several times until it flows

questions.questionText: In a C five-finger position for the right hand, finger 1 plays which note?
questions.explanation: In C five-finger position, finger 1 (the thumb) plays C, and fingers 2–5 cover D, E, F, G.

topics.title: Playing Hands Separately
topics.shortDescription: Master each hand on its own before combining them.
topics.longDescription: Most pieces should be learned hands separately first. This builds reliability in each hand and lets you focus on accuracy and fingering without the cognitive load of coordination.
objectives: Practice right-hand and left-hand parts independently
Use consistent, planned fingering
Bring each hand to a fluent level before combining
commonMistakes: Skipping hands-separate practice and jumping straight to hands together
Practicing only the right hand because it's the melody
practiceTips: If you're stuck hands-together, return to hands separately — almost always the bottleneck is in one specific hand

questions.questionText: Why practice hands separately?

options.optionText: It builds reliability in each hand before adding coordination
It's required by music theory rules

questions.explanation: Hands separately isolates each hand's challenges so you can fix problems precisely before the added cognitive load of coordination.

sections.title: Foundation Technique
sections.shortDescription: Your first scales, exercises, and the thumb-under technique that unlocks everything beyond five fingers.
sections.longDescription: This section establishes the basic technical building blocks: the C major scale, three more common keys, simple Hanon exercises, and the critical thumb-crossing technique.

topics.title: C Major Scale (One Octave, One Hand)
topics.shortDescription: The first scale you should learn — all white keys, classic fingering.
topics.longDescription: The C major scale uses only white keys, making it the ideal first scale. You'll learn the standard fingering (RH: 1-2-3-1-2-3-4-5; LH: 5-4-3-2-1-3-2-1) and play it smoothly in both hands separately.
objectives: Play C major scale ascending and descending in the right hand with correct fingering
Play C major scale ascending and descending in the left hand with correct fingering
Maintain a steady tempo with a metronome
commonMistakes: Using wrong fingering and getting away with it slowly — it breaks down at speed
Tense thumb crossing
practiceTips: Start slowly (60 BPM at quarter notes) and increase gradually
Focus especially on the thumb-under transition (RH between 3 and 1 ascending)

questions.questionText: The standard right-hand fingering for one octave of C major ascending is:
questions.explanation: RH C major ascending: 1-2-3-1-2-3-4-5. The thumb tucks under after finger 3 to continue the scale.

topics.title: G, D, and F Major Scales
topics.shortDescription: Three more one-octave scales that introduce black keys and new fingerings.
topics.longDescription: G major has one sharp (F♯), D major has two (F♯, C♯), and F major has one flat (B♭). The fingerings adjust slightly for black keys, especially in F major's left hand. These four scales cover most beginner repertoire keys.
objectives: Play G, D, and F major scales hands separately with correct fingering
Recognize the standard fingering principle: avoid putting the thumb on a black key when possible
Increase tempo gradually with the metronome
commonMistakes: Using C major fingering reflexively in keys that need adjustment
practiceTips: Drill each scale daily — variety prevents over-reliance on muscle memory in one key

assessments.title: G, D, F Major Scales Quiz

questions.questionText: A common fingering principle in piano scales is:

options.optionText: Always use the same fingering as C major
Avoid putting the thumb on a black key when possible

questions.explanation: Scale fingerings are designed to avoid putting the thumb on a black key, as black keys are less ergonomic for the thumb.

topics.title: Beginning Hanon Exercises
topics.shortDescription: Short technical patterns to develop finger evenness and strength.
topics.longDescription: Hanon's 'The Virtuoso Pianist' contains 60 exercises that drill finger independence and evenness through repetition. The first few are the most useful for beginners. They should be played slowly with full attention to tone, not just speed.
objectives: Play Hanon No. 1 hands separately with even tone
Use a metronome to verify rhythmic evenness
Avoid the temptation to rush
commonMistakes: Treating Hanon as a speed contest — speed without control teaches nothing useful
Tensing while drilling — every Hanon session should leave the hands relaxed
practiceTips: Practice Hanon in different rhythms (dotted, triplets) to vary the challenge
Five minutes daily beats 30 minutes once a week

resources.title: Hanon - The Virtuoso Pianist (IMSLP, free public domain)

questions.questionText: What is the main purpose of Hanon exercises?

options.optionText: To develop finger independence and evenness through repetitive patterns

questions.explanation: Hanon focuses on technical development through repetitive finger patterns — it's pure technique, not musical expression.

topics.title: Thumb Cross-Under and Cross-Over Technique
topics.shortDescription: Smoothly pass the thumb under (ascending) or fingers over (descending) for scales and beyond.
topics.longDescription: Scales and arpeggios beyond five notes require passing the thumb under another finger or passing fingers over the thumb. The motion should be quick, smooth, and tension-free, with the thumb traveling along the keys rather than lifting away.
objectives: Pass the thumb under fingers 3 or 4 smoothly while ascending
Pass fingers 3 or 4 over the thumb smoothly while descending
Keep the wrist level — no twisting or hopping
commonMistakes: Lifting the wrist or jerking the hand at the crossing point
Letting the thumb collapse or stick out at an awkward angle
practiceTips: Isolate just the crossing motion: play 1-2-3-1 (the crossing point) repeatedly until smooth
Watch in a mirror — the motion should look invisible

questions.questionText: When the thumb passes under during an ascending scale, the wrist should:

options.optionText: Stay relatively level with smooth motion
Drop down to push the thumb

questions.explanation: A smooth, level wrist keeps the motion ergonomic and invisible to the listener. Lifting or twisting introduces tension and audible bumps.

sections.title: Basic Chords at the Keyboard
sections.shortDescription: Play triads, chord progressions, and accompaniment patterns — the building blocks of popular and classical piano.
sections.longDescription: This section moves from individual notes to harmony. You'll play triads in both hands, learn the I-IV-V progression that underlies most popular music, develop accompaniment patterns, and read from lead sheets.

topics.title: Major and Minor Triads in Root Position
topics.shortDescription: Play three-note chords with fingers 1-3-5 in each hand.
topics.longDescription: Triads in root position use fingers 1-3-5 (or 5-3-1 in the left hand). You'll learn to play major and minor triads on every white-key root and recognize the visual pattern of each chord quality.
objectives: Play major triads on all 7 white-key roots in both hands
Play minor triads on all 7 white-key roots in both hands
Identify chord quality by feel and look
commonMistakes: Forgetting the difference between major and minor (the third is the only changing note)
Using stiff, raised fingers — let them settle naturally onto the keys
practiceTips: Say 'major' or 'minor' aloud as you play each chord — couple the sound to the label

questions.questionText: Standard fingering for a right-hand root-position triad is:
questions.explanation: Right-hand root-position triads typically use 1-3-5 (thumb, middle, pinky).

topics.title: I-IV-V Progressions
topics.shortDescription: The three primary chords in a key — the basis of countless songs.
topics.longDescription: The tonic (I), subdominant (IV), and dominant (V) chords harmonize most folk and popular songs. You'll learn to play I-IV-V in common keys with smooth voice leading using inversions to minimize hand motion.
objectives: Play I-IV-V-I progressions in C, G, F, and D major
Use inverted IV and V chords to minimize hand motion
Recognize the progression by ear in songs
commonMistakes: Always jumping back to root position — inversions are smoother
Playing the bass note an octave below mechanically instead of musically
practiceTips: Find a song you know that uses I-IV-V and play along with it
Practice the progression in all common keys, not just C

questions.questionText: In the key of C major, the I, IV, and V chords are:
questions.explanation: In C major: I = C, IV = F (4th scale degree), V = G (5th scale degree).

topics.title: Simple Accompaniment Patterns
topics.shortDescription: Turn chords into musical accompaniments: block, broken, and Alberti bass.
topics.longDescription: Once you know the chords, you can accompany a melody in many ways. Block chords play all notes at once. Broken chords play them sequentially. Alberti bass uses the pattern low-high-middle-high, common in Classical-era music.
objectives: Play block chord accompaniments
Play broken chord (arpeggiated) accompaniments
Play Alberti bass patterns
Match accompaniment style to the music's character
commonMistakes: Using only one accompaniment style for everything
practiceTips: Try playing the same progression with each style to feel the difference

questions.questionText: An Alberti bass pattern typically plays the chord notes in which order?
questions.explanation: Alberti bass typically plays: lowest note, highest note, middle note, highest note — a pattern very common in Classical-era piano music.

topics.title: Playing from Chord Symbols
topics.shortDescription: Read lead sheets and play accompaniments from chord names rather than full notation.
topics.longDescription: Lead sheets show a melody with chord symbols (C, Am, G7, etc.) above. You'll learn to interpret these symbols and improvise an accompaniment, a foundational skill for popular, jazz, and church music.
objectives: Read common chord symbols (major, minor, 7, m7, maj7, sus4)
Play accompaniment from a lead sheet
Choose voicings and patterns appropriate to the style
commonMistakes: Always playing root-position block chords (boring and rhythmically static)
Not listening to the melody while accompanying
practiceTips: Find a simple lead sheet of a song you know and learn to accompany yourself singing it

questions.questionText: The chord symbol 'Dm' indicates:
questions.explanation: An uppercase letter followed by lowercase 'm' indicates a minor chord. 'Dm' = D minor.

sections.title: Hands Together Coordination
sections.shortDescription: Combine left and right hands in increasingly independent textures.
sections.longDescription: Coordinating the two hands is one of the great challenges of piano. This section moves from simple rhythmic unison to independent rhythms and finally to the most common piano texture: a singing melody over an accompaniment.

topics.title: First Hands-Together Coordination
topics.shortDescription: Combine left and right hand parts in simple textures.
topics.longDescription: Playing hands together adds a major coordination challenge. Start with simple textures where the hands play in rhythmic unison or alternation, then progress to independent rhythms.
objectives: Play simple pieces with both hands moving in the same rhythm
Play pieces with the hands alternating (one rests while the other plays)
Bring both hands to fluency at a slow tempo before increasing speed
commonMistakes: Jumping to fast tempo too early
Practicing only the easier hand when stuck
practiceTips: Start at half your target tempo — speed comes from accurate slow practice, not from straining at full tempo

questions.questionText: The most reliable way to develop hands-together coordination is:

options.optionText: Practice fast and hope it gets cleaner
Slow practice with full control before speeding up

questions.explanation: Slow, controlled practice builds reliable coordination. Speed is a result, not the goal.

topics.title: Independent Rhythms Between Hands
topics.shortDescription: Hands playing different rhythms simultaneously.
topics.longDescription: Once basic coordination is comfortable, you'll encounter passages where the hands play independent rhythms — for example, the right hand playing eighth notes while the left plays quarter notes. This requires careful subdivision and counting.
objectives: Play passages where one hand has more notes per beat than the other
Maintain pulse using internal subdivision
Identify the common subdivision that aligns both hands
commonMistakes: Letting the simpler hand drift in tempo while focusing on the busier one
Counting out loud only at slow tempos — keep counting as you speed up
practiceTips: Find the smallest common subdivision (often the busier hand) and count in that pulse

questions.questionText: When two hands play different rhythms, the easiest way to coordinate them is:

options.optionText: Count in the smallest common subdivision
Play both hands by ear without counting

questions.explanation: Counting in the smallest common subdivision gives both hands a shared rhythmic grid to align with.

topics.title: Melody and Accompaniment Coordination
topics.shortDescription: The most common piano texture: a singing melody over an accompaniment.
topics.longDescription: In melody-and-accompaniment texture, the right hand usually plays the melody and the left hand provides the harmonic support. The challenge is keeping the melody expressive and singing while the accompaniment supports rather than distracts.
objectives: Project the melody clearly while keeping the accompaniment subordinate
Match touch and articulation to the role of each hand
Maintain steady accompaniment under an expressive melody
commonMistakes: Playing both hands at the same volume (melody disappears)
Making the accompaniment too detached or rhythmically unstable
practiceTips: Practice the melody alone, then play it with the left hand at half the dynamic level you'd otherwise use

questions.questionText: When playing melody and accompaniment together, the melody should usually be:

options.optionText: The same volume as the accompaniment
Clearly more prominent than the accompaniment

questions.explanation: The melody should project clearly above the accompaniment so the listener can hear the musical line.

sections.title: Expanded Scales and Keys
sections.shortDescription: All 12 major and minor scales, in multiple forms and ranges.
sections.longDescription: Comprehensive scale practice across all keys builds technical fluency, finger memory, and harmonic familiarity. This section covers all major and minor scales in their three forms, extending to two-octave ranges.

topics.title: All 12 Major Scales (One Octave)
topics.shortDescription: Cover all major keys including those with multiple sharps and flats.
topics.longDescription: Once C, G, D, and F major are comfortable, work through all 12 major scales. Each has its own fingering, especially in keys with many black keys (e.g., B major, D♭ major, F♯ major).
objectives: Play all 12 major scales hands separately, one octave
Memorize the standard fingering for each
Use a metronome to ensure rhythmic evenness across keys
commonMistakes: Skipping the keys with many sharps/flats because they're harder
Using inconsistent fingering between practice sessions
practiceTips: Cycle through 3-4 scales per practice session, rotating so every scale gets weekly attention

questions.questionText: Which major scale has six sharps (or equivalently, six flats as G♭ major)?
questions.explanation: F♯ major has six sharps (F♯, C♯, G♯, D♯, A♯, E♯). Enharmonically, it's the same as G♭ major (six flats).

topics.title: Natural Minor Scales
topics.shortDescription: Play the natural minor form in all 12 minor keys.
topics.longDescription: Natural minor scales share the key signature of their relative major. The fingering principles are the same as for major scales, with adjustments based on the specific pattern of black and white keys.
objectives: Play natural minor scales in all 12 keys hands separately
Recognize the relative major-minor relationship at the keyboard

questions.questionText: The relative minor of C major is:
questions.explanation: A minor is the relative minor of C major — they share a key signature with no sharps or flats.

topics.title: Harmonic and Melodic Minor Scales
topics.shortDescription: Two important variants of the minor scale.
topics.longDescription: Harmonic minor raises the 7th scale degree, creating the leading tone (and a characteristic augmented second). Melodic minor raises both the 6th and 7th ascending and reverts to natural minor descending in classical practice.
objectives: Play harmonic minor in all 12 keys
Play melodic minor (classical form) in all 12 keys
Distinguish the three forms aurally
commonMistakes: Forgetting that classical melodic minor descends as natural minor

questions.questionText: In classical practice, the melodic minor scale descending is:
questions.explanation: Classical melodic minor raises 6 and 7 ascending, then reverts to natural minor descending.

topics.title: Two-Octave Scales
topics.shortDescription: Extend scales to two octaves for greater range and technical demand.
topics.longDescription: Two-octave scales require multiple thumb crossings and reinforce the smoothness of your technique. They also build endurance and serve as a stepping stone to four-octave scales.
objectives: Play two-octave scales hands separately in all major keys
Coordinate hands together at slow to moderate tempos
Maintain even tone and tempo throughout
commonMistakes: Getting faster in the middle and slowing at the turn at the top — common problem the metronome reveals
practiceTips: Practice the turn-around at the top of the scale as its own exercise

sections.title: Arpeggios and Broken Chords
sections.shortDescription: Arpeggios — the chord-based technique that underpins much piano repertoire.
sections.longDescription: Arpeggios extend chord knowledge into a flowing technique. This section covers triad arpeggios (one and two octaves), broken chord accompaniment patterns, and seventh chord arpeggios.

topics.title: Major and Minor Triad Arpeggios
topics.shortDescription: Play chord tones consecutively across one or more octaves.
topics.longDescription: An arpeggio is a chord played one note at a time. You'll learn one-octave major and minor triad arpeggios in root position, with standard fingerings that minimize stretching.
objectives: Play one-octave triad arpeggios in all 12 major and minor keys
Use the standard fingering (RH: 1-2-3-5; LH: 5-4-2-1)
Keep the wrist relaxed across the thumb crossings

questions.questionText: An arpeggio is:

options.optionText: The notes of a chord played consecutively

questions.explanation: An arpeggio plays the notes of a chord one at a time, in succession, rather than simultaneously.

topics.title: Two-Octave Arpeggios
topics.shortDescription: Arpeggios extended over two octaves require more thumb crossings.
topics.longDescription: Two-octave arpeggios drill the thumb-crossing technique in wider leaps than scales require. The fingering follows a pattern of 1-2-3 then 1-2-3-5 (or similar, hand-dependent).
objectives: Play two-octave major and minor arpeggios with smooth thumb crossings
Maintain a quiet hand position despite the wider spans
commonMistakes: Jerking the wrist at the thumb crossing
practiceTips: The wrist should move laterally, not vertically — like sliding, not hopping

topics.title: Broken Chord Patterns
topics.shortDescription: Stylized arpeggio patterns used in accompaniment (e.g., Alberti, waltz, etudes).
topics.longDescription: Beyond simple arpeggios, music uses many stylized broken chord figures: Alberti bass, waltz bass, etude patterns, and others. Mastering these unlocks vast amounts of repertoire.
objectives: Play Alberti bass smoothly with the left hand
Play waltz bass (low-chord-chord) patterns
Maintain steady accompaniment under a melody

topics.title: Dominant and Diminished Seventh Arpeggios
topics.shortDescription: Arpeggios of common seventh chords for technical and harmonic versatility.
topics.longDescription: Dominant seventh and diminished seventh arpeggios appear constantly in repertoire from Bach onward. They use slightly different fingerings than triad arpeggios because of the added note.
objectives: Play dominant seventh arpeggios in all 12 keys
Play diminished seventh arpeggios (which repeat every minor third)
Use them as warm-ups and reference patterns

sections.title: Pedaling
sections.shortDescription: Use the pedals to add resonance, legato, and color.
sections.longDescription: The pedals are the soul of expressive piano playing. This section covers the sustain pedal (basics through advanced legato pedaling) and the less-discussed una corda and sostenuto pedals.

topics.title: The Sustain Pedal: Basics
topics.shortDescription: What the rightmost pedal does and how to use it minimally at first.
topics.longDescription: The sustain pedal (rightmost on most pianos) lifts the dampers off the strings, letting notes ring even after you release the keys. Used well, it adds resonance and legato. Used poorly, it muddies everything.
objectives: Operate the pedal with the ball of the foot, heel on the floor
Press the pedal cleanly without 'clicking' the mechanism
Recognize when the pedal is over-applied (muddy sound)
commonMistakes: Holding the pedal down through chord changes (creates harmonic mud)
'Stamping' the pedal rather than smoothly depressing it
practiceTips: First learn to play passages without pedal — pedal should enhance, not cover, your finger legato

questions.questionText: What does the sustain pedal do mechanically?

options.optionText: Lifts the dampers off the strings, letting notes ring

questions.explanation: The sustain pedal lifts all the dampers, so any played notes continue to ring until the pedal is released.

topics.title: Rhythmic (Direct) Pedaling
topics.shortDescription: Press the pedal at the same time as a note or chord.
topics.longDescription: Rhythmic pedaling depresses the pedal simultaneously with playing a note. It's the simplest approach and appropriate when notes don't need to overlap.
objectives: Coordinate pedal presses with downbeats and chord changes
Lift the pedal cleanly on the change to avoid blurring

topics.title: Legato (Syncopated) Pedaling
topics.shortDescription: The technique of pressing the pedal just after a chord change for seamless connection.
topics.longDescription: Legato or syncopated pedaling depresses the pedal slightly after the chord change so that the new chord is captured cleanly while the old one is released. This is the dominant pedaling technique in classical music.
objectives: Coordinate pedal lifts with finger attacks (pedal up as hand goes down)
Apply legato pedaling to chord-progression passages
Avoid blurring chord changes
commonMistakes: Lifting the pedal too late (creates blur) or too early (loses connection)
practiceTips: Listen carefully — clean pedaling is something you train your ears to want, not just your foot to do

questions.questionText: In legato (syncopated) pedaling, the pedal is pressed:

options.optionText: At the same time as the new chord
Just after the new chord is played

questions.explanation: Legato pedaling lifts the pedal as the new chord is struck, then re-depresses it just after — capturing the new harmony cleanly.

topics.title: The Una Corda and Sostenuto Pedals
topics.shortDescription: The left and middle pedals — less common but important.
topics.longDescription: The left pedal (una corda) shifts the action so the hammers strike fewer strings, producing a softer, more muted tone — it's not just a volume control. The middle pedal (sostenuto on grands) holds only those notes already depressed when it's pressed, allowing selective sustain.
objectives: Understand the mechanical action of each pedal
Use the una corda for tonal color, not just volume
Use the sostenuto when the score calls for selective sustain
commonMistakes: Treating the una corda as a 'quiet button' — it changes timbre as much as volume

sections.title: Musical Expression
sections.shortDescription: Dynamics, articulation, phrasing, voicing, and rubato — what turns notes into music.
sections.longDescription: Technique gets you to the notes; expression makes them mean something. This section covers dynamics, articulation, phrasing, voicing, and the tasteful use of rubato.

topics.title: Dynamics at the Keyboard
topics.shortDescription: Produce a wide dynamic range with control.
topics.longDescription: Dynamics on piano come from the speed of the hammer striking the string, which is controlled by how quickly you depress the key. You'll learn to play from pianissimo (pp) to fortissimo (ff) with control, and to balance hands so the melody projects.
objectives: Play single notes and chords across a wide dynamic range
Balance hands so the melody is louder than the accompaniment
Make smooth crescendos and decrescendos
commonMistakes: Banging at fortissimo (loses tone — speed of attack matters more than force)
Producing tense soft playing instead of true pianissimo (relaxation is key for ppp)
practiceTips: Practice an entire piece pp, then ff — you'll find untapped dynamic range in both directions

questions.questionText: Piano dynamics are primarily controlled by:

options.optionText: How fast you depress the key

questions.explanation: Volume on a piano comes from the speed of the hammer striking the string, which depends on how quickly you press the key down.

topics.title: Articulation: Legato, Staccato, Tenuto
topics.shortDescription: Connect or separate notes for character and clarity.
topics.longDescription: Articulation is how notes are connected or separated. Legato connects notes smoothly. Staccato shortens them, creating space. Tenuto sustains the full value. Other articulations include marcato (accented) and portato (between legato and staccato).
objectives: Play smooth legato lines
Produce clean staccato of varying lengths
Recognize and execute articulation markings
commonMistakes: Staccato that's too short and harsh ('clipped')
Legato that breaks because of careless finger release
practiceTips: Sing a phrase to find the natural articulation, then transfer that to the keys

questions.questionText: Legato means:
questions.explanation: Legato indicates that notes should flow smoothly into each other without separation.

topics.title: Phrasing and Voicing
topics.shortDescription: Shape musical lines and bring out important inner voices.
topics.longDescription: Phrasing is the shape of a musical line — rising and falling in intensity like speech. Voicing means bringing out one voice in a multi-voice texture, usually by playing it louder than the others.
objectives: Shape phrases with dynamics and timing
Voice a melody above accompanying notes in the same hand
Bring out an inner voice from a chord
commonMistakes: Playing every note at the same volume (no phrasing)
Equalizing all voices in a chord (no voicing)
practiceTips: Practice the melody in isolation, then play the chord and listen for the same melody to project

topics.title: Tempo and Rubato
topics.shortDescription: Sustain a steady tempo, then learn when and how to bend it musically.
topics.longDescription: First, master steady tempo with a metronome. Then learn rubato — the controlled stretching and compression of tempo for expression. Rubato is not random; it follows musical instinct and stylistic convention.
objectives: Maintain steady tempo across long passages
Use rubato tastefully in appropriate styles (Chopin, Schumann, etc.)
Avoid rubato in styles that demand strict tempo (Bach, ragtime)
commonMistakes: Slowing down at hard passages — rubato should be expressive, not avoidant
Constant rubato — undifferentiated stretching loses its meaning

sections.title: Sight-Reading
sections.shortDescription: The skill of playing unfamiliar music at sight — a separate, ongoing discipline.
sections.longDescription: Sight-reading is a parallel skill that develops alongside everything else. This section teaches the fundamentals, advocates for daily practice, and progresses to reading ahead and recognizing patterns at a glance.

topics.title: Sight-Reading Fundamentals
topics.shortDescription: Play music you've never seen before, at a steady tempo, without stopping.
topics.longDescription: Sight-reading means playing music at first sight. The core skill is keeping going — not stopping to fix mistakes — and reading ahead of where you're playing. Start with material well below your performance level.
objectives: Play through unfamiliar simple music without stopping
Maintain tempo even when mistakes occur
Read ahead by at least one beat
commonMistakes: Stopping to fix every wrong note (kills the flow and the skill)
Sight-reading at your performance level (too hard — sight-read below your level)
practiceTips: Sight-read daily, even for just 5 minutes. Use a wide variety of material.
Before playing, scan the score: key, time signature, problem spots, hand positions

questions.questionText: When sight-reading, what should you do when you make a mistake?

options.optionText: Stop and fix it before continuing
Keep going at tempo and ignore the mistake

questions.explanation: The core sight-reading skill is to keep going. Stopping for every mistake builds the wrong habit; the goal is to maintain flow.

topics.title: Daily Sight-Reading Practice
topics.shortDescription: Make sight-reading a daily, ongoing habit.
topics.longDescription: The single most effective way to improve at sight-reading is to do it daily. Use a variety of material — hymns, simple sonatinas, four-hand duets, popular tunes — at a level well below your performance pieces.
objectives: Practice sight-reading every day, even briefly
Vary the material across styles and textures
Track progress over weeks and months
practiceTips: Hymnals and easy sonatina collections are gold for daily sight-reading material
Pair with a sight-reading partner for accountability and duet material

topics.title: Reading Ahead and Pattern Recognition
topics.shortDescription: Look ahead of where you're playing and recognize patterns at a glance.
topics.longDescription: Advanced sight-readers look several beats ahead of where they're playing, and they recognize patterns (scales, arpeggios, common rhythms, common chord progressions) at a glance rather than reading each note. Pattern recognition is what makes sight-reading feel easy.
objectives: Train your eye to look at least one measure ahead
Recognize scales, arpeggios, and common patterns by their visual shape
Read chords as units, not note-by-note
commonMistakes: Reading note-by-note instead of in patterns
practiceTips: Cover the measure you're currently playing — force yourself to look ahead
Time how long you can keep playing before your eyes get pulled back

sections.title: Chord Inversions at the Keyboard
sections.shortDescription: Make chords flow smoothly through inversions and voicings.
sections.longDescription: Inversions transform chord knowledge into smooth musical motion. This section covers triad inversions, voice-leading through them, and seventh chord voicings used in popular music and jazz.

topics.title: Triad Inversions on the Piano
topics.shortDescription: Play first and second inversions in both hands.
topics.longDescription: Triad inversions place the third or fifth in the bass instead of the root. At the keyboard, you'll learn the visual shape of root position (close together) versus first inversion (a gap between the bottom two notes) and second inversion (a gap between the top two).
objectives: Play all three positions of any triad in both hands
Recognize inversions visually on the keys
Transition between inversions smoothly
commonMistakes: Treating inversions as different chords (they're the same chord rearranged)
practiceTips: Practice cycling through R-1st-2nd-R for each chord you learn — fingering and feel both lock in

questions.questionText: A C major triad in first inversion has which note on the bottom?
questions.explanation: First inversion places the third on the bottom. The third of C major is E, so first inversion is E-G-C from the bottom.

topics.title: Smooth Voice Leading Through Inversions
topics.shortDescription: Use inversions to move between chords with minimal hand motion.
topics.longDescription: Inversions exist largely so you can move between chords smoothly. Instead of jumping the whole hand for each chord, you can use the inversion whose notes are closest to the previous chord.
objectives: Play I-IV-V-I with smooth voice leading (using inversions)
Apply the principle: move to the closest available chord position
Apply this to lead sheet accompaniment
commonMistakes: Reverting to root position every chord (creates choppy accompaniment)

topics.title: Seventh Chord Voicings
topics.shortDescription: Play common seventh chord voicings used in popular music and jazz.
topics.longDescription: Seventh chords are four notes (1-3-5-7), too many for a single hand to play comfortably along with a melody. Pianists use partial voicings (often omitting the root, or splitting voicings between hands) to make them practical.
objectives: Play 'shell' voicings (root, 3rd, 7th) common in jazz comping
Play full seventh chord voicings with both hands
Apply to a simple jazz tune
practiceTips: Practice voicings in all 12 keys before applying to a specific tune

sections.title: Intermediate Technique
sections.shortDescription: Czerny etudes, multi-octave scales, octaves, ornaments, and hand crossing.
sections.longDescription: This section extends technical foundations into intermediate territory. Czerny etudes, multi-octave scales, octave technique, ornaments, and hand crossing each address a specific technical challenge.

topics.title: Czerny and Advanced Hanon
topics.shortDescription: Etudes and exercises that build technical fluency at the intermediate level.
topics.longDescription: Carl Czerny wrote hundreds of progressive etudes that drill specific technical challenges in musical contexts. Op. 599, Op. 849, and Op. 299 are commonly used. Advanced Hanon (later exercises, played in different keys and rhythms) extends the basic exercises.
objectives: Work through selected Czerny etudes appropriate to your level
Apply advanced Hanon variations (different keys, rhythms, articulations)
Use these for warm-up rather than the entire practice session

resources.title: Czerny - Op. 599 Practical Method for Beginners (IMSLP, free)
Czerny - Op. 849, 30 New Studies in Technique (IMSLP, free)
Czerny - Op. 299 School of Velocity (IMSLP, free)

topics.title: Scales in Multiple Octaves
topics.shortDescription: Extend to four-octave scales hands together.
topics.longDescription: Four-octave scales hands together are a staple of intermediate and advanced technique. They build endurance, evenness, and ensemble between the hands across the full keyboard.
objectives: Play four-octave scales hands together in all major keys
Maintain even tone, tempo, and finger articulation
Apply to harmonic and melodic minor as well
commonMistakes: Letting the volume swell at the extremes of the keyboard

topics.title: Octave Technique
topics.shortDescription: Play octaves with control, speed, and minimal tension.
topics.longDescription: Octaves use fingers 1 and 5 simultaneously, an octave apart. They appear constantly in intermediate and advanced repertoire. The challenge is keeping the hand relaxed during fast or repeated octave passages.
objectives: Play smooth octave passages legato and staccato
Use wrist motion rather than arm force for fast octaves
Recognize and avoid tension
commonMistakes: Locking the wrist — the wrist must stay flexible
Pushing through tension instead of stopping to relax
practiceTips: Practice octaves in short bursts with relaxation breaks
If your hand tightens up, stop. Tension practiced is tension learned.

topics.title: Trills and Ornaments
topics.shortDescription: Mordents, turns, trills, and grace notes.
topics.longDescription: Ornaments embellish a main note. Trills alternate rapidly between adjacent notes. Mordents and turns are short ornamental figures. Practice and stylistic interpretation matter — Baroque ornaments follow different conventions than Classical or Romantic.
objectives: Play clean, even trills
Execute upper and lower mordents
Recognize and play turns
Adjust ornament interpretation to historical style

topics.title: Hand Crossing
topics.shortDescription: One hand reaching over or under the other for melodic continuity.
topics.longDescription: Hand crossing is common in intermediate repertoire. The crossing hand needs to know exactly where it's landing without looking. Spatial awareness and confident motion are key.
objectives: Cross hands accurately without looking
Maintain musical flow through the crossing point
Plan crossings in advance based on the score
commonMistakes: Looking at the target — train the hand to find it by feel
practiceTips: Practice the crossing motion in isolation, hand alone, eyes closed

sections.title: Intermediate Repertoire
sections.shortDescription: Bach, Classical sonatinas, Romantic character pieces, and popular works at the intermediate level.
sections.longDescription: Repertoire is where everything comes together. This section guides you through intermediate Bach (Anna Magdalena, Inventions), Classical sonatinas (Clementi, Kuhlau), Romantic character pieces (Schumann, Tchaikovsky), and popular/contemporary intermediate works.

topics.title: Bach: Anna Magdalena Notebook and Two-Part Inventions
topics.shortDescription: Foundational Baroque keyboard works.
topics.longDescription: Bach's Notebook for Anna Magdalena contains accessible pieces (Minuets, Marches) that introduce his style. The Two-Part Inventions are the next step: short pieces where each hand plays an equal, often imitative line. Both are essential intermediate Bach.
objectives: Play several pieces from the Anna Magdalena Notebook
Begin work on selected Two-Part Inventions
Develop hand independence required for two-voice counterpoint
commonMistakes: Treating the left hand as accompaniment in inventions — both voices are equal
practiceTips: Practice each voice separately and learn it as if it were a melody alone
Sing one voice while playing the other

resources.title: Notebook for Anna Magdalena Bach (IMSLP, free)

topics.title: Classical Sonatinas
topics.shortDescription: Clementi, Kuhlau, and Diabelli sonatinas — accessible Classical-era works.
topics.longDescription: Sonatinas are smaller-scale sonatas. Clementi's Op. 36 and Kuhlau's Op. 20 and 55 are staples of intermediate repertoire, teaching Classical phrasing, Alberti bass, and clear textures.
objectives: Learn at least 2-3 sonatinas from this repertoire
Apply Classical-style phrasing and articulation
Master the Alberti bass texture

topics.title: Romantic Character Pieces
topics.shortDescription: Schumann, Tchaikovsky, and others for the young/intermediate player.
topics.longDescription: Schumann's Album for the Young Op. 68 and Tchaikovsky's Album for the Young Op. 39 contain Romantic-era miniatures suitable for intermediate players. They develop musical character, dynamic range, and rubato.
objectives: Learn several character pieces from these collections
Develop expressive variety across distinct character pieces
Apply rubato tastefully

resources.title: Schumann - Album for the Young Op. 68 (IMSLP, free)

topics.title: Popular and Contemporary Intermediate Pieces
topics.shortDescription: Film music, popular ballads, simple jazz tunes — match the era to your interest.
topics.longDescription: Beyond the classical canon, the intermediate player has a vast world of popular, film, and contemporary repertoire. Choose pieces aligned with your interests: Ludovico Einaudi, film themes, simplified pop arrangements, jazz standards, or your favorite songs.
objectives: Choose 2-3 pieces aligned with your musical interests
Apply the same practice discipline as classical repertoire
Use these as motivation and breadth — not as a replacement for foundational study
commonMistakes: Skipping foundational work because popular pieces are 'more fun' — both kinds of repertoire have their place

sections.title: Improvisation
sections.shortDescription: Invent music spontaneously — over scales, progressions, blues, and freely.
sections.longDescription: Improvisation extends your musicianship beyond reading scores. This section progresses from scale-based improvisation through chord progressions, the blues, and finally free improvisation.

topics.title: Improvising with Scales
topics.shortDescription: Begin to invent melodies over a static or simple harmonic background.
topics.longDescription: Improvisation starts with playing within a scale. Choose a key, set up a drone or simple bass line, and explore melodic ideas using the scale. The goal is fluency and creative freedom, not virtuosity.
objectives: Improvise melodic phrases within a single major or minor scale
Listen to and respond to your own ideas (call and response)
Use rhythmic variety as well as melodic variety
commonMistakes: Playing only running eighth notes — vary the rhythm
Trying to play 'right' instead of 'musical'
practiceTips: Record yourself and listen back — discover what works

topics.title: Improvising Over Chord Progressions
topics.shortDescription: Layer melodic improvisation over a moving harmonic background.
topics.longDescription: Once you can improvise over a static harmony, extend to progressions: I-IV-V-I, ii-V-I, blues. Listen to which scale notes work over each chord and which create tension/resolution.
objectives: Improvise over I-IV-V-I and ii-V-I
Target chord tones on strong beats
Use chromatic approaches and passing tones
practiceTips: Loop a progression in software or with a backing track and improvise for 10+ minutes daily

topics.title: Blues at the Piano
topics.shortDescription: The 12-bar blues form and basic blues improvisation.
topics.longDescription: The 12-bar blues is a foundational form: I-I-I-I-IV-IV-I-I-V-IV-I-V (with variations). Learn the form, the blues scale, and basic comping patterns. Blues develops feel, swing, and improvisation in one package.
objectives: Play a 12-bar blues progression with the left hand
Improvise over a blues using the blues scale
Apply blue notes (♭3, ♭5, ♭7) tastefully
commonMistakes: Playing the blues scale up and down repetitively — break it up into phrases
practiceTips: Find a backing track and just play. Listening time on blues is as important as practice time.

questions.questionText: A standard 12-bar blues uses how many distinct chords?
questions.explanation: A basic 12-bar blues uses three chords: I, IV, and V (typically as dominant 7ths in a blues context).

topics.title: Free Improvisation
topics.shortDescription: Invent music without a predetermined structure.
topics.longDescription: Free improvisation isn't 'random' — it draws on every musical skill you have, but without the constraint of a fixed form or harmonic plan. It's a way to develop your personal voice and apply theory creatively.
objectives: Improvise without a predetermined form for several minutes at a time
Use motifs and development
Listen and shape larger arcs
commonMistakes: Playing technical patterns rather than musical ideas
practiceTips: Set a timer and improvise for the entire time — no stopping, no judging

sections.title: Advanced Technique
sections.shortDescription: Scales in intervals, polyrhythms, advanced octaves, and endurance.
sections.longDescription: This section pushes technical skill into advanced territory: scales in thirds and sixths, polyrhythms, sustained octave passages, and building the endurance demanded by advanced repertoire.

topics.title: Scales in Thirds, Sixths, and Octaves
topics.shortDescription: Play scales with both hands a third, sixth, or octave apart, or in one hand simultaneously.
topics.longDescription: Advanced scale technique includes playing scales in parallel intervals (thirds, sixths, octaves). One-hand scales in thirds or sixths are particularly demanding because two fingers must play together evenly.
objectives: Play hands-together scales in thirds, sixths, and octaves
Begin one-hand scales in thirds (intermediate-advanced repertoire requirement)
Maintain evenness and tempo
practiceTips: These take months or years to develop — patience is essential

topics.title: Polyrhythms
topics.shortDescription: Two against three, three against four, and other independent rhythmic patterns.
topics.longDescription: Polyrhythms (two simultaneous rhythms that don't share a common subdivision) are common in late Romantic and modern repertoire. Two-against-three is the most common. The skill is keeping both rhythms independent without one influencing the other.
objectives: Play 2-against-3 cleanly with hands independent
Play 3-against-4 in pieces that require it
Practice with a metronome until both rhythms feel natural
practiceTips: Verbalize the cross-rhythm pattern (e.g., 'Nice cup of tea' for 2-against-3) until it's automatic

topics.title: Advanced Octave Work
topics.shortDescription: Sustained fast octave passages, octave glissandi, and ergonomic strategies.
topics.longDescription: Advanced octave work appears in Liszt, Brahms, and Tchaikovsky concertos, among others. Endurance, relaxation between notes, and wrist suppleness are critical to avoid injury.
objectives: Play extended fast octave passages with relaxation
Build endurance gradually
Recognize and respond to fatigue before it becomes injury
commonMistakes: Pushing through pain or fatigue — this is how injuries happen

topics.title: Building Endurance and Stamina
topics.shortDescription: Develop the physical and mental endurance for long, demanding pieces.
topics.longDescription: Advanced repertoire often demands 20–40 minutes of intense playing. Endurance comes from consistent practice habits, smart pacing, and physical conditioning. Recovery between practice sessions is as important as the practice itself.
objectives: Gradually extend practice duration without tension or injury
Apply targeted endurance practice to demanding pieces
Maintain mental focus across long performances
commonMistakes: Marathon practice sessions without breaks (counterproductive and risky)
practiceTips: Several shorter, focused sessions usually beat one long session
Stretch and rest hands between intense practice blocks

sections.title: Advanced Repertoire
sections.shortDescription: Bach WTC, Classical sonatas, Romantic, 20th-century, jazz and popular advanced piano.
sections.longDescription: The advanced repertoire opens the full piano canon. This section guides you through Bach's WTC and Partitas, Classical sonatas (Haydn, Mozart, Beethoven), Romantic works (Chopin, Schumann, Brahms, Liszt), 20th-century music, and advanced jazz and popular piano.

topics.title: Bach: Well-Tempered Clavier and Partitas
topics.shortDescription: Bach's mature keyboard masterworks.
topics.longDescription: The Well-Tempered Clavier (two books of 24 preludes and fugues each, one in every major and minor key) and the Partitas are pinnacles of Baroque keyboard music. They demand polyphonic clarity, structural awareness, and finger independence.
objectives: Learn several preludes and fugues from the WTC
Work on at least one Partita movement
Develop the polyphonic hearing required for fugue performance
practiceTips: Memorize each voice separately before combining
Listen to multiple interpretations to hear stylistic possibilities

topics.title: Classical Sonatas
topics.shortDescription: Haydn, Mozart, and Beethoven sonatas.
topics.longDescription: The Classical sonata is the central genre for the advanced pianist. Haydn's sonatas (often underrated) develop wit and clarity. Mozart's demand absolute control and singing tone. Beethoven's range from accessible (Op. 49) to monumental (the late sonatas).
objectives: Learn at least one complete sonata from each of these composers
Master sonata form across multiple movements
Apply Classical-style phrasing, articulation, and pedaling

topics.title: Romantic Era Repertoire
topics.shortDescription: Chopin, Schumann, Brahms, Liszt, and others.
topics.longDescription: Romantic-era repertoire is vast and varied: Chopin's nocturnes, etudes, and ballades; Schumann's character cycles; Brahms's intimate and weighty piano works; Liszt's transcendental virtuosity. Each composer demands a slightly different technical and expressive approach.
objectives: Learn pieces from at least two Romantic-era composers
Develop appropriate stylistic rubato and pedaling
Manage the technical demands (octaves, leaps, fast passagework)

topics.title: 20th-Century and Contemporary Repertoire
topics.shortDescription: Debussy, Ravel, Bartók, Prokofiev, Messiaen, and beyond.
topics.longDescription: 20th-century piano music encompasses Impressionism (Debussy, Ravel), neoclassicism (Stravinsky, Prokofiev), nationalism (Bartók), serialism, minimalism, and many other currents. Each demands a specific approach.
objectives: Learn at least one work from a 20th-century or contemporary composer
Adapt your tonal palette and pedaling to the style
Engage with non-tonal harmonic languages

topics.title: Advanced Jazz and Popular Piano
topics.shortDescription: Jazz standards, stride, modern jazz, and advanced popular arrangements.
topics.longDescription: Advanced jazz piano involves comping, soloing, stride and ragtime styles, modern modal and chromatic playing, and the language of bebop and beyond. Popular advanced repertoire ranges from complex film and game music to virtuoso arrangements.
objectives: Build a working repertoire of jazz standards
Develop comping and soloing skills over chord changes
Explore stylistic variety in popular and contemporary piano
practiceTips: Transcribe solos by ear from recordings — the single best way to internalize jazz language

sections.title: Ear Training and Memorization for Pianists
sections.shortDescription: Play by ear, transcribe, and memorize reliably — skills that develop in parallel.
sections.longDescription: This section covers three parallel skills critical for the well-rounded pianist: playing by ear, transcription, and memorization. They develop over years alongside everything else.

topics.title: Playing by Ear
topics.shortDescription: Reproduce melodies and harmonies at the piano without sheet music.
topics.longDescription: Playing by ear means hearing music and translating it to the keyboard. Start with simple melodies in a known key, then add bass lines, then chord progressions. This skill connects your hearing directly to your hands.
objectives: Play simple melodies by ear in a given key
Add a basic bass line to a melody you've worked out
Identify chord progressions by ear and play them
commonMistakes: Searching for notes at random — listen first, then play
practiceTips: Practice with melodies you know extremely well — the easier it is to recall, the easier to find on the keys
Start with one finger, one note at a time — don't try to play full textures first

topics.title: Transcription Practice
topics.shortDescription: Write down what you hear — a deep ear-training discipline.
topics.longDescription: Transcription combines ear training, music theory, and notation. You'll choose a short recording (a melody, a solo, a chord progression) and notate it as accurately as possible. The slow, careful listening involved trains your ear faster than almost any other activity.
objectives: Transcribe a simple melody from recording
Add chord changes from a recording
Extend to a short solo or passage
practiceTips: Use software that lets you slow down recordings without changing pitch
Write down what you hear before checking the score

topics.title: Memorization Techniques
topics.shortDescription: Reliable memorization for performance.
topics.longDescription: Memorization is essential for serious performance. Robust memory combines four kinds: muscle memory (hands know where to go), aural memory (you can hear it in your head), visual memory (you see the score in your mind), and analytical memory (you understand the structure).
objectives: Memorize pieces using multiple memory modes
Test memory by starting from different points (not just the beginning)
Maintain memorized pieces through periodic review
commonMistakes: Relying only on muscle memory — it breaks down under pressure
Never testing memory by starting in the middle of the piece
practiceTips: Mentally play through a piece away from the piano — if you can do that, your memory is solid
Practice starting from every section, every page, every difficult spot
